activities_projects.title: Critical Analysis of Rutherford’s Postulates
activities_projects.objective: To correlate experimental observations with the mathematical probability of alpha-particle deflection.
materials: Detailed diagram of Alpha-particle scattering experiment
steps: Analyze the observation that 1 out of 12,000 particles rebounded at 180 degrees.
Calculate the approximate volume of the nucleus relative to the atom based on these statistics.
Construct a diagram showing the concentrated positive charge and the vast empty space as proposed by Rutherford.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes how a single rare observation led to the discovery of the atomic nucleus and the overthrow of the Thomson model.
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why the rebound of alpha particles was described as 'firing a 15-inch shell at a piece of tissue paper and it comes back and hits you' (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Stability Paradox: Maxwell’s Theory vs. Rutherford
activities_projects.objective: To investigate the theoretical failure of the nuclear model regarding electromagnetic radiation.
materials: Research notes on Maxwell’s electromagnetic theory
steps: Examine the theory that any accelerated charged particle must radiate energy.
Model the spiral path an electron would take if it lost energy while revolving around the nucleus.
Discuss how Bohr’s concept of 'discrete orbits' resolved the paradox of atomic collapse.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the necessity of quantized energy levels in maintaining atomic stability.
activities_projects.evaluation: Provide a technical argument for why Rutherford’s atom should be unstable according to classical physics (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Bohr-Bury Configuration Logic
activities_projects.objective: To derive the electronic distribution of elements based on energetic stability.
steps: Apply the $2n^2$ rule for the first four shells (K, L, M, N).
Analyze the 'Octet Rule' constraint for the outermost shell regardless of $n$.
Derive the step-wise filling order for Potassium (Z=19) and Calcium (Z=20) where the N-shell begins before the M-shell is full.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Mastery of advanced electronic configuration and the principles of shell capacity.
activities_projects.evaluation: Determine the electronic configuration of an element with atomic number 18 and explain its stability (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Valency and Chemical Reactivity Trends
activities_projects.objective: To correlate the number of valence electrons with the periodic nature of reactivity.
materials: Schematic structures of the first 18 elements
steps: Compare the valence shells of Helium, Neon, and Argon.
Identify why elements with 1 or 7 valence electrons are highly reactive compared to those with full shells.
Calculate the valency of Phosphorus and Sulphur, explaining why they exhibit multiple valencies (e.g., Phosphorus as 3 or 5).
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that chemical activity is a drive toward a stable octet configuration.
activities_projects.evaluation: Differentiate between 'valence electrons' and 'valency' using Magnesium and Nitrogen as examples (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Mass Spectrometry Concept: Average Atomic Mass
activities_projects.objective: To calculate the fractional atomic mass of elements based on isotopic abundance.
materials: Abundance data for Chlorine and Carbon isotopes
steps: Use the data: Cl-35 (75%) and Cl-37 (25%).
Formulate the weighted average equation to find the relative atomic mass.
Research why the mass of a single atom of Chlorine is never 35.5 u, yet the average mass is.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Mathematical proficiency in calculating isotopic distributions and understanding fractional mass units.
activities_projects.evaluation: Calculate the average atomic mass of an element X existing as 90% of ${}_{8}^{16}X$ and 10% of ${}_{8}^{18}X$ (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: The Neutron Discovery and Nucleon Balance
activities_projects.objective: To examine the contribution of J. Chadwick and the concept of mass number.
steps: Discuss the discovery of the neutron in 1932 and its location in the nucleus.
Calculate the number of neutrons in different isotopes of Hydrogen (Protium, Deuterium, Tritium).
Analyze why the mass of an atom is concentrated in the nucleus by comparing proton and electron masses.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the definition of mass number (A) as the total number of protons and neutrons (nucleons).
activities_projects.evaluation: An atom has mass number 4 and 2 protons. Determine the number of neutrons and name the element (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Isotopes vs. Isobars: Structural Divergence
activities_projects.objective: To contrast nuclear variants based on atomic and mass numbers.
materials: Data sets for Calcium (Z=20, A=40) and Argon (Z=18, A=40)
steps: Compare the number of protons and neutrons in Calcium and Argon.
Define Isobars as atoms of different elements with the same mass number.
Contrast these with Isotopes of Carbon (C-12 and C-14) regarding their chemical properties.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Distinguishes between elements that share mass vs. elements that share identity.
activities_projects.evaluation: Compare isotopes and isobars based on their chemical reactivity and sub-atomic counts (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Industrial and Medical Radiochemistry
activities_projects.objective: To evaluate the specific properties of isotopes in technology.
materials: Case studies on Uranium-235, Cobalt-60, and Iodine isotopes
steps: Investigate how an isotope of Uranium is used as fuel in nuclear reactors.
Research the role of the Cobalt isotope in cancer therapy and the Iodine isotope in treating goitre.
Identify why isotopes of the same element have similar chemical properties but different physical properties.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Appreciates the practical application of atomic structure in modern science and medicine.
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why the chemical properties of isotopes remain identical despite different masses (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Composition of the Nucleus and Notation
activities_projects.objective: To master the standard notation ${}_{Z}^{A}X$ for atoms and ions.
steps: Practice writing the notation for the Oxide ion ($O^{2-}$) and Sodium ion ($Na^{+}$).
Analyze the change in the number of electrons vs. the stability of the nucleus during ion formation.
Verify that the mass number remains unchanged during chemical ion formation.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that chemical reactions involve valence electrons, while the nucleus remains intact.
activities_projects.evaluation: If an ion $X^{2-}$ has 10 electrons and 8 neutrons, calculate the atomic number and mass number of X (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Atomic Theory Comparison Project
activities_projects.objective: To construct a timeline of the evolution of atomic models.
materials: Summaries of Thomson, Rutherford, and Bohr models
steps: Compare the location and behavior of electrons in all three models.
Analyze the discovery of the three sub-atomic particles: electron (Thomson), proton (Goldstein/Rutherford), and neutron (Chadwick).
Summarize how each model improved upon the previous one's limitations.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Provides a comprehensive overview of the historical development of atomic structure.
activities_projects.evaluation: Compare all the proposed models of an atom given in this chapter in a tabular form (10 marks).
